perturbation.original_text: “Trade Secrets ” means any information held by Room2Rent that is or could be used industrially or commercially, is not generally available, has potential economic value, and is the subject of reasonable efforts by Room2Rent to preserve its secrecy .
perturbation.changed_text: “Trade Secrets ” means any information held by Room2Rent, regardless of its potential commercial use, general availability, or economic value, and whether or not Room2Rent makes reasonable efforts to preserve its secrecy.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of "Trade Secrets" removes key elements required for information to be legally classified as a trade secret, specifically contradicting the requirement for commercial value, secrecy, and efforts to maintain secrecy. This could render the trade secret protections in the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Zealand Trade Secrets Act (Crimes Act 1961, Section 230)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 230 of the Crimes Act 1961 relates to the theft of trade secrets. The modified definition contradicts the implied understanding within Section 230 that a trade secret must possess qualities of confidentiality and economic value. By removing these qualities, the contract attempts to protect information that would not qualify as a trade secret under New Zealand law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Crimes Act 1961 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Act of Parliament in New Zealand For a list of Acts in other countries, see Crimes Act . Crimes Act New Zealand Parliament Long title An Act to consolidate and amend the Crimes Act 1908 and certain other enactments of the Parliament of New Zealand relating to crimes and other offences Royal assent 1 November 1961 Commenced 1 January 1962 Legislative history Passed 1961 Amended by Homosexual Law Reform Act 1986 Prostitution Reform Act 2003 Crimes (Substituted Section 59) Amendment Act 2007 Crimes Amendment Act (No 2) 2008 Abortion Legislation Act 2020 Related legislation Criminal Procedure Act 2011 Sentencing Act 2002 Status: Amended The Crimes Act 1961 is an act of New Zealand Parliament that forms a leading part of the criminal law in New Zealand. It repeals the Crimes Act 1908, [ 1 ] itself a successor of the Criminal Code Act 1893. [ 2 ] Most crimes in New Zealand are created by the Crimes Act, but some are created elsewhere. All common law offences are abolished by section 9, as are all offences against acts of the British Parliaments , [ 1 ] but section 20 saves the old common law defences where they are not specifically altered. [ 1 ] The Crimes Act is administered by the Ministry of Justice . The act has 14 parts dealing with various issues including jurisdiction, punishments, "matters of justification and excuse", crimes against the public order, crimes affecting the administration of law and justice, "crimes against morality and decency, sexual crimes, and crimes against public welfare", "crimes against the person", property crimes, and "threatening, conspiring, and attempting to commit offences." [ 3 ] Over the years, the legislation has been amended by several new acts, including the Homosexual Law Reform Act 1986 , the Prostitution Reform Act 2003 , the Crimes (Substituted Section 59) Amendment Act 2007 ("anti-smacking law"), the Crimes Amendment Act (No 2) 2008, and the Abortion Legislat
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines "Trade Secrets" by removing the requirements for commercial use, general non-availability, economic value, and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. This directly contradicts the generally understood and legally implied components of a trade secret. While the provided snippets do not directly define 'trade secret,' the Law Explanation correctly identifies that Section 230 of the Crimes Act 1961 implies that a trade secret possesses qualities of confidentiality and economic value. By removing these qualities, the modified contract attempts to protect information that would likely not qualify as a trade secret under law, thus creating a legal contradiction because it broadens the scope beyond what would likely be legally protected.

perturbation.original_text: “Representative ” means any officer or employee of the Recipient, or any adviser engaged by the Recipient in connection with the Due Diligence and to whom the Recipient is permitted to disclose the Confidential Information under Clause 3.1.
perturbation.changed_text: “Representative ” means any individual with whom the Recipient shares Confidential Information, irrespective of their connection to the Due Diligence or any prior consent from Room2Rent.
perturbation.explanation: This modification broadens the definition of 'Representative' to include any individual receiving confidential information, removing the requirements for connection to Due Diligence and prior consent. This conflicts with Clause 3.1, which outlines specific conditions for disclosure to Representatives and could lead to unauthorized disclosures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017 (NZ), subpart 1, section 24.
perturbation.law_citation: Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017, subpart 1, section 24
perturbation.law_explanation: Subpart 1, section 24 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017 addresses contractual capacity and rights. By broadly defining Representative and removing the due diligence connection and consent requirements, the modified text undermines Room2Rent's ability to control the dissemination of its Confidential Information, potentially violating their rights as the disclosing party, particularly if these representatives are acting outside the scope of an agreement or relationship. The new definition does not account for the duties representatives owe, which could lead to liability issues not addressed in the contract.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly broadens the definition of 'Representative,' removing the original requirements tied to Due Diligence and prior consent from Room2Rent. This directly contradicts the original contract's intention to control the dissemination of Confidential Information. While no specific law snippet is provided, the Law Explanation indicates this change undermines Room2Rent's rights as the disclosing party and could lead to liability issues due to the lack of defined duties for these newly defined 'Representatives'. The original text provided safeguards, whereas the changed text opens up the possibility of unauthorized disclosure, creating a legal contradiction by misaligning the term 'Representative' with its intended contractual meaning, making enforcement of confidentiality clauses more difficult.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will hold all Confidential Infor mation in strict confidence and will not disclose any Confidential Information to any person, ex cept in accordance with the explicit provisions of this Agreement or, in any other case, only on receiving the prior written consent of Room2Rent .
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will endeavor to hold Confidential Information in reasonable confidence and may disclose Confidential Information to any person, without prior consent from Room2Rent, if the Recipient deems it necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'strict confidence' to 'reasonable confidence' and allowing disclosure without prior consent weakens the confidentiality obligation. This directly contradicts the intended protection of the Confidential Information and increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tort of Breach of Confidence (Common Law - New Zealand)
perturbation.law_citation: Tort of Breach of Confidence (Common Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: New Zealand's common law tort of breach of confidence establishes a legal duty to maintain confidentiality where such a duty exists. The modified text, by weakening the obligation to 'reasonable confidence' and permitting disclosure without consent 'if the Recipient deems it necessary,' undermines the basis for a claim of breach of confidence. The 'necessity' exception is too broad and subjective, potentially allowing for disclosures that would otherwise constitute a breach under common law principles.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandated 'strict confidence' and required 'prior written consent' before disclosing Confidential Information. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'reasonable confidence' and permits disclosure 'if the Recipient deems it necessary,' thus removing the prior consent requirement. This contradicts the common law tort of breach of confidence, as it undermines the legal duty to maintain confidentiality. The 'necessity' exception is too broad and subjective, which could allow for disclosures that would otherwise constitute a breach under common law principles.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient acknowledges tha t it understands that theft of T rade Secrets is an offence under section 230 of the Crimes Ac t 1961, and can be punishable by a term of up to five years imprisonment.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient acknowledges that it understands that unintentional disclosure of information vaguely resembling Trade Secrets may result in civil penalties.
perturbation.explanation: This modification drastically alters the acknowledgement regarding trade secret theft. It changes a criminal offense to a potential civil penalty for unintentional disclosure, significantly downplaying the legal consequences of misappropriating trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Crimes Act 1961, Section 230 (Theft of Trade Secrets)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 230 of the Crimes Act 1961 clearly defines the theft of trade secrets as a criminal offense punishable by imprisonment. The altered text contradicts this by framing the issue as a mere civil matter related to 'unintentional disclosure,' thus misrepresenting the severe legal ramifications associated with trade secret theft under New Zealand law.

Crimes Act New Zealand Parliament Long title An Act to consolidate and amend the Crimes Act 1908 and certain other enactments of the Parliament of New Zealand relating to crimes and other offences Royal assent 1 November 1961 Commenced 1 January 1962 Legislative history Passed 1961 Amended by Homosexual Law Reform Act 1986 Prostitution Reform Act 2003 Crimes (Substituted Section 59) Amendment Act 2007 Crimes Amendment Act (No 2) 2008 Abortion Legislation Act 2020 Related legislation Criminal Procedure Act 2011 Sentencing Act 2002 Status: Amended The Crimes Act 1961 is an act of New Zealand Parliament that forms a leading part of the criminal law in New Zealand. It repeals the Crimes Act 1908, [ 1 ] itself a successor of the Criminal Code Act 1893. [ 2 ] Most crimes in New Zealand are created by the Crimes Act, but some are created elsewhere. All common law offences are abolished by section 9, as are all offences against acts of the British Parliaments , [ 1 ] but section 20 saves the old common law defences where they are not specifically altered. [ 1 ] The Crimes Act is administered by the Ministry of Justice . The act has 14 parts dealing with various issues including jurisdiction, punishments, "matters of justification and excuse", crimes against the public order, crimes affecting the administration of law and justice, "crimes against morality and decency, sexual crimes, and crimes against public welfare", "crimes against the person", property crimes, and "threatening, conspiring, and attempting to commit offences." perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the law because it changes the consequence of trade secret theft from a criminal offense punishable by imprisonment to a potential civil penalty for unintentional disclosure. The original text acknowledges theft of trade secrets as an offense under section 230 of the Crimes Act 1961, punishable by up to five years imprisonment. The changed text downplays the legal consequences of misappropriating trade secrets. The LLM Law Explanation supports this by stating Section 230 of the Crimes Act 1961 clearly defines the theft of trade secrets as a criminal offense punishable by imprisonment. The provided Wikipedia snippet of the Crimes Act 1961 describes it as an act of New Zealand Parliament that forms a leading part of the criminal law in New Zealand.
